data.text:
INT: Tape seven of Mark Verstandig. We were talking the previous discussion about your time at university, and you talked about some of the far right-wing students and the trouble they caused. When you look back at the day-to-day experience with other students in general, was there any ethnic tension or religious conflict at university? 

MV: At university, yes. That extension was in Cracow. And the attitude of the Polish students against the Jews. But again, I wouldn't say the Polish students as a whole. I mean the so-called [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE], they were the troublemakers. 

The other Poles were indifferent. No, I-- I have-- I can't remember a Pole coming out in defense of the Jews. But they were indifferent, but they didn't take part in it. Whether they like it-- liked it or not. Maybe they didn't. But it was, like always, a minority of troublemakers, a minority to-- which was seeking power riding on the Jewish horse. And that was a very convenient horse. 

INT: Where do you think this prominence of the far right-wing students came from? 

MV: The prominence came-- to a certain extent, there was always trouble from the beginning of the-- from the beginning of the Polish independence. The Polish independence, as I mentioned earlier, was created-- I mean, the-- with the Polish independence, there were pogroms all over Poland. All over Poland. 

It does not mean that the population was eager to pogrom Jews. In Galicia, for instance, in our town, the population came in because they were told to-- the farmers were told that they will be able to loot, to get something. But it so happened they came for three weeks. In the third week, I could see the-- the-- the government sent in some military. And our balcony, I remember-- I was six years old-- there was a machine gun. 

And I remember that there were-- I have seen because I was on the balcony, as a child, a six-year-old child, and I-- I could see the-- like today, I remember it. There were three soldiers, and they were hitting the Poles with the butt of the rifle. Was not much. But then suddenly there were-- there-- they could see smoke coming out-- coming out from two different directions in the villages, enclosed by villages. From one side and from the other side. 

And within minutes, the Poles, the-- the peasant disappeared because they could see that somebody burned this-- this-- this-- their houses somewhere could be on fire. It so happens that [INAUDIBLE] it was later I heard that there were a few people, young people, who-- who were in the Austrian army. They were serving in the Austrian army, and they organized for the-- it was the third week. They organized some kind of a self-defense. 

They placed themselves during the night in a couple of villages. In one village on the north and the other on the south, for instance. And they put on fire a couple of barns. And that was enough to keep the Poles away-- away from-- from-- from town. There were no more pogroms whatsoever. In other towns, it was much-- this was worse. But the worst pogroms during-- connected with the-- in the Polish independence were instigated by army officers. Just like that, by army officers. 

For instance, in Vilna. In Vilna, they arrested a number of Jews. The army arrested a number of Jews. And they did the same as the SS did 20 years later, 23 years later. They-- they-- they took them out and told them to dig their own graves. 

A famous Jewish writer was shot. He was arrested together with another, the Jewish poet-- poet Yaffe. I don't remember the name of the Jewish writer. He was a Bundist. I-- I'll-- I could-- I could check on it. They shot him at that time. Yaffe was arrested. Yaffe was a Hebrew poet, a Zionist, a Hebrew poet. And eventually he was relieved and thrown. 

But there were pogroms in Vilna. In Lindbergh, in Lwow, there were terrible pogroms. Because the officers said that the Jews should help-- should come out against the Ukrainians. Because the Jews did not come-- come out against the Ukrainians, there were pogroms of Jews and there-- quite a number of Jews. A very big number of Jews were killed. 

So what I am driving at to say is that it was not the population of Poles. There were the instigators. So Polish officers in the army, mostly Polish officers in the army in the beginning. And later was the element-- the elements of the [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE]. They instigated Poles to do-- to-- to-- to do the same as Hitler did in Kristallnacht later. 

So that was the problem. The minority-- a minority of Poles poisoned the atmosphere. Take, for instance, the establishment of the Polish-- of-- of the Polish Republic. 

As you know-- as you know, the moment the Poles established themselves, Pilsudski marched against Kiev and conquered Kiev. You-- you-- you know about it. They entered Kiev. They went over there to Ukraine to establish the Polish boundaries from the 16th century, including the Ukraine as it should belong to Poland together with Kiev. 

Now, who went over there? The legionnaires of-- of Pilsudski, and they were to-- they were going together with Haller's army-- General Joseph Haller, Jozef Haller. Jozef Haller established himself a name in the Jewish history. They were killing and-- and beating up Jews and tearing the beards of the Jews out with the flesh. They did it in towns, in shtetls, and they did it in-- in-- in the trains. 

And what is interesting, they came to the-- Haller's army came to Poland under the leadership of French officers. But the fact is that the French officers did not take part in the pogroms. Now, Haller himself, his army, although Poland was a sovereign state, his army came in on the condition that it will not be under the Pilsudski regime. It will be like a sovereign-- like an independent-- an independent army, under the French officers. 

But the French officers [INAUDIBLE] didn't take back-- didn't take-- didn't pogrom Jews. Only the Haller-- Haller's army. That is well-known. In Poland, they used to say Haller chicken. That means they-- they-- they used to tear out the beard together with the flesh. 

Anyhow, that's-- but what is not very well-known is that Pilsudski made to use an alliance with Petliura. And you know-- you remember Petliura? Hetman Petliura. The man who was shot later by a Jew in Paris that he organized the pogroms, the most bloody pogroms in Ukraine-- in the Ukraine, and the French court acquitted him. He was a Hitler. 

And Pilsudski went to Kiev together with General Haller and together-- and together with Hetman Petliura. In the Polish-- matter of fact, in the Polish history you never-- they will-- they never mentioned the shameless story-- stories. I discovered it recently on the internet. On the internet. And it was-- the internet quoted-- it was an essay published in the Gazeta Polska. 

Wouldn't have-- if it-- if it wouldn't have been the truth, Gazeta Polska wouldn't have dared to connect Petliura with Pilsudski. Now, in the end, he left-- Pilsudski came back, and he left the [INAUDIBLE] over there in Kiev. You remember him? Rydz-Smigly. Rydz-Smigly was a Polish [INAUDIBLE] in Kiev. 

In the meantime, Budyonny marched on Kiev on the one side, and took [NON-ENGLISH] from the north, and Poland suddenly became squished, and they marched. They were on the outskirt of [NON-ENGLISH]. Polish independence was just hanging on the-- on-- on a hair. 

And at that time, the Polish government-- the prime minister was Witos, Wincenty Witos, The leader of the Piast. Piast, peasant party, or the [INAUDIBLE] farmers' party. He was the prime minister. He was from-- from our neighborhood. He was from Tarnow, from-- from a-- from a village close to Tarnow, between Tarnow and Mielec. 

And his deputy was Ignacy Daszynski, who was the leader of the PPS, of the Socialist Party of Poland. They issued an appeal, a-- an appeal through the people of Poland to the intelligentsia, and to the people of Poland. Whoever served in the-- in the Austrian army, whoever served in the German army, in Russian army, and feels that he is a Pole, that he is-- that feels that a Polish independence is-- is really in danger, he should volunteer-- he should volunteer to help defend Poland. 

About 10,000 Jewish intellectuals, most of them former officers of the Prussian-- of the-- of the Austrian army and of the Prussian army and of the Russian army, most of them. But there were some youngsters who never served in the army. They all volunteered, 10,000 of them. 

And what happened? They came to the point of-- to-- to-- to the-- to the point showing indicators that they should come over there. They all were arrested, and they were put into a concentration camp in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the first concentration camp of Jews, Jews who volunteered to defend Poland. 

As a matter of fact, here in Melbourne, if you go to the cemetery, you will see that some 15 months ago or something like that died a man, 95 years old, Dr. Handelsman. 

Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH] Handelsman. He was there. He was a 20-year-old man, and he was there in this camp. Now, Dr. Handelsman was a scholar, was later a French citizen. I-- he showed me his photographs with the-- with-- with men in France, with-- with-- with-- with a number of Poles, a number of-- of French politicians and so on. He studied in Paris, in Sorbonne, and Sorbonne published a few books of his in French. 

Then before he-- matter of fact, before he died-- before he died he phoned me that he want to show me some documents. I remembered him, but I didn't remember his age. I remembered the name. I told him, all right. So come along. I live here and-- said, look, I'm 95 years old. 95 years old. I can't travel. Said, if you are 95, I'll come to see you. 

He shows me all the documents of-- all the documents relating to the [NON-ENGLISH], and all the correspondence he had with people like-- who were like him in-- in this [NON-ENGLISH]-- in this [NON-ENGLISH] concentration camp. After quite a time-- quite a time, a few years, they were released because there was an outcry in the-- in the whole world. So it was the Polish-- it was the-- the prime-- by the way, this appeal that they should come along, it was signed-- no. 

The document to arrest them was signed by General Sosnkowski, the-- the-- he-- Pilsudski's deputy. So you see, there was a kind of a-- of an anti-Jewish approach. They thought that the Jews are-- that-- that the Jews will-- will be in the army and will help-- will help the Bolsheviks. Now, that is how it started. 

Then Poland was already established. There was-- no more was-- was peace with Russia. And the boundaries were established. We are not in Kiev, but the-- but the borders were established. Everything was right. But then there was the Sejm. 

And Pilsudski realized-- at that time he was [NON-ENGLISH]. It means the-- he was the head of the state. And there was this constitution, and then he realized that the majority in the Sejm are always against him. The majority of the Sejm were the peasant party, the [NON-ENGLISH], and the [NON-ENGLISH], the Christian Democrats, the national-- the-- the antisemitic national party [NON-ENGLISH], and the anti-Jewish party that was the party of-- of-- of-- of-- of-- of the peasants-- what is-- led by Wincenty Witos. 

He realized that the majority is against him. So he withdrew. And the government was a government of [INAUDIBLE]. Later was the Grabskis. Yes. With the Grabskis-- with Wladyslaw Grabski and Stanislaw Grabski. One was a minister for culture, for education. And the other was minister-- the-- the other was a treasurer. And then they were in government, and they were anti-Jewish. 

And although the Constitution of the 3rd of May of 1922 was democratic and contained all-- all kinds of democratic stipulations, but it was on paper only. What they first of all did is to gerrymander through geography. 

I mean, this an example. If Mielec had, let's say, 5,000 Jews, and Tarnow had 40,000 Jews, and that is one close to the other, what they did is they joined Mielec with a different region where they were a few hundred Jews, where-- where Jews did not live. 

That means that this Mielec, Tarnow, Jaslo, and so on couldn't-- couldn't elect a deputy to the-- to the-- to the parliament because geographically, for-- for the sake of elections, [INAUDIBLE] constituencies was created in such a case-- in-- in-- in such a way that people that-- cities with Jews were-- were cut off, were connected with large-- with-- with large territories, villages, where there are no Jews whatsoever. So they were not able to elect anybody. Only, let us say, a few people here, Cracow, and where the Jews themselves had enough votes to elect somebody. 

And then came Greenbaum with an idea. This is what they did not only to the-- the Jews, but they did similar things to-- to-- to the Ukrainians. So Greenbaum, being a deputy, organized a-- a coalition between the Jews, Ukrainians, the Germans. 

All ethnics were joined together in a coalition. And they decided that if an Ukrainian will stand for election in a place where there are Jews and Ukrainians, the Jews will vote for Ukrainian. If the majority there are Ukrainian, the Jews will vote for Ukrainian. 

And in places where there were more Jews than Ukrainians, the Ukrainians will vote for the Jews. And the whole gerrymander collapsed. If Poland wanted to hide that 35% to 37% of the population are minorities, and in the [NON-ENGLISH] they committed themselves to give all the rights to the minorities. If they wanted to hide the problem that Poland consists of only 65%-- the most, 62%, 63%, the most 65% of Poles, and 35% are minorities. They wanted to hide it with-- through this gerrymander. 

Here suddenly the Jews alone came up with 35-- with 35 deputies in parliament with a number of-- with a number of senators. I mean, the real number-- the number of the-- of the ethnic representatives in the Polish parliament in the lower house and in the-- and in the senate really reflected the real population percentage in the country. It was something, you see, they didn't expect to. 

Then with that up, they came-- there was a-- there-- there was a-- there-- they came up with an idea of the so-called Ugoda, which means with an idea to make an agreement with the Jews. This happened on a Friday night. As I mentioned to you, there was in Cracow a Jew called Leon Selpeter, was a friend of my brother. Comes from [POLISH], was a pharmacist and a-- a great Zionist. 

He is mentioned, by the way, in Schindler's Ark-- in-- in Schindler's Ark as the man who gave Schindler letters to the Jewish Congress, the old Jewish Congress. He was in Schindler's Ark. 

So Leon Selpeter told the story secretly, in-- in-- in still secrecy told it to my brother. And-- and, oh, the story went up from one-- from mouth to mouth, houses-- from houses [INAUDIBLE]. It was a Friday night. And before Friday, Stanislaw Markowski-- Stanislaw Grabski. Stanislaw Grabski suggested to Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], who was the leader of the-- of the-- of the Jews in Galicia. He suggested to him that he would like to come to see him on Friday night for so-called Jewish fish, for gefilte fish, which you will-- well, there was very-- they were-- the-- the Poles very much liked Jewish fish. 

So he invited himself to Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] for the Jewish-- for gefilte fish. And Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] invited him and there was this great-- this-- this reception, the kiddush and so on, and the gefilte fish. But after the fish, Stanislaw Grabski came out with an idea that the Jews should make an agreement with the government. The agreement be Ugoda, the agreement being that the Jews will declare their-- you call it [NON-ENGLISH]. Their-- 

INT: Citizenship? 

MV: Huh? 

INT: Citizenship. 

MV: No. They declare their loyalty. They will-- should declare their loyalty to the Polish Republic in the borders which are now-- in the present borders, and in-- and for that, the government will see what can be done to improve the Jewish Polish relations. 

Surely Greenbaum was against it. And so were a number of Jews. They said first of all why should be the Jews singled out, that they should declare loyalty to Poland in the present boundaries? The present boundaries were disputed by the Ukrainians. That means they wanted to put a [INAUDIBLE] between the Jews and the Ukrainians. The present boundaries were not recognized by the-- by-- by the League of Nations. 

So why should the Jews be singled out? That's number one. And number two, the promise was nothing. We will see what we can do to improve the Polish Jewish relations. It was a great-- there was a great affair in the Jewish-- in the Jewish community all over Poland. 

There was the-- Greenbaum in Warsaw was against it, and there was Dr. [INAUDIBLE] who was always in [INAUDIBLE]. Galicia was always trying to-- not to fight, not to fight. To try-- to get out whatever is possible, although he never believes that the Poles will-- will-- will stick to the constitution. 

INT: What year was that in? 

MV: 1922, '23. 

INT: Thank you. We'll have to finish next tape.

value.text: Constitution of the 3rd of May of 1922
